title: Benchmarking Subset Aggregation for Classical Causal Discovery Under Marginalization Error
description: This project is an empirical benchmark of fixed-variable subset aggregation for classical CPDAG discovery under a strict CPU-only budget. It holds one bank of local subset PC-Stable CPDAGs fixed, compares compatibility-based weighting against uniform and bootstrap-stability controls, and audits how often local subset claims already disagree with the true induced subgraph before interpreting any global gain.
motivation: Novelty is too weak for a new-method claim because Self-Compatibility, LOVO, SEA, DAGBag, higher-order aggregation, and consistency-guided aggregation already cover most of the conceptual space. The real gap is a careful benchmark that asks whether overlap-compatibility weighting is empirically useful in fixed-variable subset aggregation once the marginalization soundness problem is treated as a first-class concern rather than ignored.
proposed_approach: Generate one shared local graph bank per dataset using PC-Stable on M=20 overlapping subsets of size k=8 with B=2 row bootstraps. Extract only explicit CPDAG claims, define compatibility penalties from explicit overlap contradictions while treating abstention as neutral, compare exponential, rank-based, and top-half compatibility weighting against uniform and within-subset bootstrap-stability controls, and merge claims with both Wilson-style and deterministic rules. Separately, for every sampled subset, compare the local learned claims to the true induced-subgraph CPDAG so the paper can report subset distortion before attributing any aggregation improvement to meaningful causal signal.

related_work.title: Self-Compatibility: Evaluating Causal Discovery without Ground Truth
related_work.authors: Philipp M. Faller, Leena C. Vankadara, Atalanti A. Mastakouri, Francesco Locatello, Dominik Janzing
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the closest conceptual precursor because it already uses subset disagreement as an internal reliability signal. The proposed paper does not introduce a new compatibility principle; it benchmarks one concrete use of that signal as an aggregation weight.

related_work.title: Cross-validating causal discovery via Leave-One-Variable-Out
related_work.authors: Daniela Schkoda, Philipp M. Faller, Patrick Blobaum, Dominik Janzing
related_work.how_it_differs: LOVO is a global model-selection criterion rather than a subset aggregation method. In the refined proposal it appears only as a baseline selector, not as local evidence inside the shared subset bank.

related_work.title: Sample, estimate, aggregate: A recipe for causal discovery foundation models
related_work.authors: Menghua Wu, Yujia Bao, Regina Barzilay, Tommi Jaakkola
related_work.how_it_differs: SEA already establishes that subset-estimate-aggregate pipelines can work, but with learned neural aggregation and much larger compute. This proposal isolates a symbolic, CPU-only benchmark question instead of proposing another aggregation framework.

related_work.title: Causal discovery on vector-valued variables and consistency-guided aggregation
related_work.authors: Urmi Ninad, Jonas Wahl, Andreas Gerhardus, Jakob Runge
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the main novelty threat because it already studies consistency-guided aggregation and its failure modes. The proposed paper is differentiated only by its narrower empirical setting: fixed observed variables, overlapping subset CPDAGs, and an explicit audit of marginalization-induced distortion.

related_work.title: Learning directed acyclic graphs via bootstrap aggregating
related_work.how_it_differs: DAGBag is the key classical aggregation baseline in the literature. The proposal is not the first to aggregate learned graphs; it studies a different ensemble source, namely overlapping subset CPDAGs on a fixed variable set.

related_work.title: Bootstrap aggregation and confidence measures to improve time series causal discovery
related_work.authors: Kevin Debeire, Andreas Gerhardus, Jakob Runge, Veronika Eyring
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that bagging and aggregation can help in time-series causal discovery, but not this fixed-variable static CPDAG subset benchmark.

related_work.title: Causal Discovery on Higher-Order Interactions
related_work.authors: Alessio Zanga, Marco Scutari, Fabio Stella
related_work.how_it_differs: This narrows novelty for simple claim-frequency aggregation by arguing that higher-order structure matters. The proposal therefore frames its contribution as a benchmark of weighting choices, not as a new aggregation theory.

related_work.title: AutoCD: Automated Machine Learning for Causal Discovery Algorithms
related_work.authors: Gerlise Chan, Tom Claassen, Holger H. Hoos, Tom Heskes, Mitra Baratchi
related_work.how_it_differs: Represents the alternative paradigm of selecting a single global learner or hyperparameter setting instead of aggregating local graphs.

related_work.title: Intersection-Validation: A Method for Evaluating Structure Learning without Ground Truth
related_work.authors: Jussi Viinikka, Ralf Eggeling, Mikko Koivisto
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides older agreement-based evaluation without ground truth, but not fixed-bank subset aggregation with a marginalization audit.

hypothesis: On p=20 synthetic finite-sample stress tests, compatibility-based weighting of overlapping local CPDAGs will improve orientation reliability over uniform weighting in the harder regimes, and at least part of that effect will survive one deterministic merger and outperform a non-falsification bootstrap-stability control. Any such gain will be interpreted as empirical utility only when the subset-distortion audit shows the local bank is noisy but not overwhelmingly inconsistent with the true induced subgraphs.

confirm: Compatibility weighting lowers median false-orientation rate by at least 10% relative to uniform weighting on the 48-dataset benchmark under at least one merger family.
The sign of the compatibility-weighting effect is consistent under both Wilson-style and at least one deterministic merger on near-unfaithful or mildly misspecified regimes.
Compatibility weighting matches or exceeds the bootstrap-stability control under the same merger, so gains are not explained by generic local stability alone.
The subset-distortion audit shows that apparent gains are not produced merely by extreme sparsity or near-total abstention on orientations.
The full benchmark plus reduced-slice sensitivity analyses remain feasible within the 2-core, CPU-only, roughly 8-hour budget.
refute: Any apparent advantage disappears once the Wilson-style merger is replaced by deterministic merging.
Uniform or bootstrap-stability weighting matches compatibility weighting across the hard regimes.
The subset-distortion audit shows severe disagreement with induced-subgraph truth exactly where compatibility weighting appears to help, making the gain hard to interpret as meaningful signal.
Strong full-graph baselines such as FGES, NOTEARS-L1, or self-compatibility-based selection achieve the same or better improvement with less complexity.
